title: Structural Anchors and Reasoning Fragility:Understanding CoT Robustness in LLM4Code
abstract: This paper examines the robustness of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting in Large Language Models for Code (LLM4Code), identifying three trajectory deformations (Lengthening, Branching, and Simplification) and suggesting design principles for more robust reasoning-based code generators.

methodology.approach: Analysis of how perturbations interact with structural anchors in reasoning trajectories
methodology.focus: Examining failure modes under different perturbations and measuring predictive power of early-stage uncertainty
methodology.metrics: AUROC (Area Under Receiver Operating Characteristic) for uncertainty prediction

key_findings: CoT is not universally beneficial for code generation robustness
Its value depends on how perturbations interact with structurally sensitive anchors
When anchors are destabilized, reasoning trajectories exhibit specific patterns (Lengthening, Branching, Simplification) that correlate with failures
Early-stage uncertainty is weakly predictive of final correctness (AUROC ≈ 0.55–0.60) but serves as a diagnostic signal
introduction_highlights: LLM4Code models like CodeLlama, DeepSeek-Coder, and Qwen have shown impressive capabilities but remain unstable under prompt variations
Robust reasoning is crucial for agentic software engineering systems that interact with external tools
Minor linguistic perturbations can drastically change reasoning behavior and output correctness
